original_abstract: In recent years, scholarship has demonstrated that the American public engages with foreign policy through relatively stable orientations toward international affairs, shaped by a blend of demographic, ideological, and partisan factors. Despite growing speculation regarding the role of religion in defining these worldviews, rigorous empirical research on the subject remains scarce. This article addresses this gap by utilizing the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs survey to map American religious groups onto the classic dimensions of foreign policy attitudes identified by Wittkopf (1990): militant internationalism and cooperative internationalism. The findings reveal distinct religious constituencies for each perspective. Evangelical Protestants and religious traditionalists across various faiths emerge as the primary supporters of militant internationalism, whereas ethnoreligious minorities and religious modernists are significantly more likely to favor cooperative internationalism.
humanized_abstract: Prior research has identified relatively stable public attitudes toward international issues that are influenced by a variety of demographic, ideological, and partisan factors. There has been much speculation about the potential role of religion in foreign policy attitudes but relatively little empirical investigation. This article redresses this balance by using the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs survey of the American public to map the various religious groups in the United States onto Wittkopf's militant—cooperative dimension of foreign policy attitudes. The results show that each of the worldviews identified by Wittkopf has its own religious constituency: militant internationalism is strongly identified with Evangelical Protestants and with religious traditionalists in other faiths, while ethnoreligious minorities and religious modernists in the three major Western religions are all disproportionately identified with the cooperative world view.

The results show that each of the worldviews identified by Wittkopf has its own religious constituency: militant internationalism is strongly identified with Evangelical Protestants and with religious traditionalists in other faiths, while ethnoreligious minorities and religious modernists in the three major Western religions are all disproportionately identified with the cooperative world view.
